messages.content:
You are hired as a contractor to help a university migrate an existing student system to a new platform using C++ language. Since the application already exists, its requirements exist as well, and they are outlined in the next section. You are responsible for implementing the part of the system based on these requirements. A list of data is provided as part of these requirements. This part of the system is responsible for reading and manipulating the provided data.


You must write a program containing two classes (i.e., Student and Roster). The program will maintain a current roster of students within a given course. Student data for the program include student ID, first name, last name, email address, age, an array of the number of days to complete each course, and degree program. This information can be found in the â€śstudentData Tableâ€ť below. The program will read a list of five students and use function calls to manipulate data (see part F4 in the requirements below). While parsing the list of data, the program should create student objects. The entire student list will be stored in one array of students called classRosterArray. Specific data-related output will be directed to the console.

 

studentData Table

 

Student ID
	

First Name
	

Last Name
	

Email
	

Age
	

Days in Course
	

Degree Program

A1
	

John
	

Smith
	

John1989@gm ail.com
	

20
	

30, 35, 40
	

SECURITY

A2
	

Suzan
	

Erickson
	

Erickson_1990@gmailcom
	

19
	

50, 30, 40
	

NETWORK

A3
	

Jack
	

Napoli
	

The_lawyer99yahoo.com
	

19
	

20, 40, 33
	

SOFTWARE

A4
	

Erin
	

Black
	

Erin.black@comcast.net
	

22
	

50, 58, 40
	

SECURITY

A5
	

Your first name
	

Your last name
	

Your valid email address
	

Your age
	

Number of days to complete 3 courses
	

SOFTWARE

 

The data should be input as follows:

 

const string studentData[] = 

{"A1,John,Smith,John1989@gm ail.com,20,30,35,40,SECURITY", "A2,Suzan,Erickson,Erickson_1990@gmailcom,19,50,30,40,NETWORK", "A3,Jack,Napoli,The_lawyer99yahoo.com,19,20,40,33,SOFTWARE", "A4,Erin,Black,Erin.black@comcast.net,22,50,58,40,SECURITY", "A5,[firstname],[lastname],[emailaddress],[age], [numberofdaystocomplete3courses],SOFTWARE"
A.  Modify the â€śstudentData Tableâ€ť to include your personal information as the last item.
 

B.  Create a C++ project in your integrated development environment (IDE) with the following files:

â€˘  degree.h

â€˘  student.h and student.cpp

â€˘  roster.h and roster.cpp

â€˘  main.cpp
 

Note: There must be a total of six source code files.
 

C.  Define an enumerated data type DegreeProgram for the degree programs containing the data type values SECURITY, NETWORK, and SOFTWARE.
 

Note: This information should be included in the degree.h file.
 

D.  For the Student class, do the following:

1.  Create the class Student  in the files student.h and student.cpp, which includes each of the following variables:

â€˘  student ID

â€˘  first name

â€˘   last name

â€˘  email address

â€˘  age

â€˘  array of number of days to complete each course

â€˘  degree program

2.  Create each of the following functions in the Student class:

a.  an accessor (i.e., getter) for each instance variable from part D1

b.  a mutator (i.e., setter) for each instance variable from part D1

c.  All external access and changes to any instance variables of the Student class must be done using accessor and mutator functions.

d.  constructor using all of the input parameters provided in the table

e.  print() to print specific student data
 

E.  Create a Roster class (roster.cpp) by doing the following:

1.  Create an array of pointers, classRosterArray, to hold the data provided in the â€śstudentData Table.â€ť

2.  Create a student object for each student in the data table and populate classRosterArray.

a.  Parse each set of data identified in the â€śstudentData Table.â€ť

b.  Add each student object to classRosterArray.

3.  Define the following functions:

a.  public void add(string studentID, string firstName, string lastName, string emailAddress, int age, int daysInCourse1, int daysInCourse2, int daysInCourse3, DegreeProgram degreeProgram)  that sets the instance variables from part D1 and updates the roster.

b.  public void remove(string studentID)  that removes students from the roster by student ID. If the student ID does not exist, the function prints an error message indicating that the student was not found.

c. public void printAll() that prints a complete tab-separated list of student data in the provided format: A1 [tab] First Name: John [tab] Last Name: Smith [tab] Age: 20 [tab]daysInCourse: {35, 40, 55} Degree Program: Security. The printAll() function should loop through all the students in classRosterArray and call the print() function for each student.

d.  public void printAverageDaysInCourse(string studentID)  that correctly prints a studentâ€™s average number of days in the three courses. The student is identified by the studentID parameter.

e.  public void printInvalidEmails() that verifies student email addresses and displays all invalid email addresses to the user.
 

Note: A valid email should include an at sign ('@') and period ('.') and should not include a space (' ').
 

f.  public void printByDegreeProgram(DegreeProgram degreeProgram) that prints out student information for a degree program specified by an enumerated type.
 

F.  Demonstrate the programâ€™s required functionality by adding a main() function in main.cpp, which will contain the required function calls to achieve the following results:

1.  Print out to the screen, via your application, the course title, the programming language used, your WGU student ID, and your name.

2.  Create an instance of the Roster class called classRoster.

3.  Add each student to classRoster.

4.  Convert the following pseudo code to complete the rest of the  main() function:

classRoster.printAll();

classRoster.printInvalidEmails();

 

//loop through classRosterArray and for each element:

classRoster.printAverageDaysInCourse(/*current_object's student id*/);


Note: For the current_object's student id, use an accessor (i.e., getter) for the classRosterArray to access the student id.


classRoster.printByDegreeProgram(SOFTWARE);

classRoster.remove("A3");

classRoster.printAll();

classRoster.remove("A3");

//expected: the above line should print a message saying such a student with this ID was not found.

5.  Implement the destructor to release the memory that was allocated dynamically in Roster.

This is a rubric meant to help youRubric:
A:Personal Information
Personal information in the last item of the â€śstudentData Tableâ€ť is complete.
B:C++ Project
The C++ project is correctly created in the IDE and correctly uses the given files.
C:Enumerated Data Type
The enumerated data type is correctly defined.
D1:Class Student
The class Student is correctly created by correctly including each of the given variables.
D2a:Accessor
The accessor function in the Student class for each instance variable from part D1 is functional and is complete.
D2b:Mutator
The mutator function in the Student class for each instance variable from part D1 is functional or is complete.
D2c:Accessor and Mutator Usage
External access and changes to all instance variables of the Student class are done using accessor and mutator functions.
D2d:Constructor
The constructor function in the Student class accurately uses all of the input parameters from the data table.
D2e:Printing Specific Data
The print() function in the Student class accurately prints specific student data.
E1:Array
The array of pointers created to hold the data provided in the â€śstudentData Tableâ€ť is complete and is correct.
E2:Student Object
The student object for each student in the classRosterArray is correctly populated.
E2a:Parsed Data
The data is parsed correctly.
E2b:Adding Student Objects to Array
Each student object is correctly added to the classRosterArray.
E3a:Add Function
The public void add(string studentID, string firstName, string lastName, string emailAddress, int age, int daysInCourse1, int daysInCourse2, int daysInCourse3,DegreeProgram degreeProgram) function is correctly defined to set the instance variables from part D1 and update the roster.
E3b:Remove Function
The public void remove(string studentID) that removes students from the roster by student ID is correctly defined.
E3c:Print All Function
The public void printAll() prints a complete tab-separated list of student data. The printAll() correctly loops through all of the students in the student list and correctly calls the print() function for each student.
E3d:Print Average Function
A public void printAverageDaysInCourse(string studentID) correctly prints a studentâ€™s average number of days in the 3 courses by student ID. The student is correctly identified by the student-ID parameter.
E3e:Print Invalid Emails Function
A public void printInvalidEmails() correctly verifies student email addresses and displays all invalid email addresses to the user.
E3f:Print Degree Program Function
A public void printByDegreeProgram (DegreeProgram degreeProgram) correctly prints out student information for a degree program specified by an enumerated type.
F1:Screen Print Out
The course title, programming language used, WGU student ID, and student name are all correctly printed at the top.
F2:Roster Class Instance
The instance of the Roster class called classRoster is correctly created.
F3:Add Students
All students are added to classRoster .
F4:Pseudo Code Conversion
All pseudo code is correctly converted to complete the rest of the main() function and is in order.
F5:Destructor
The Roster memory is released by implementing the destructor.
G:Professional Communication
Content reflects attention to detail, is organized, and focuses on the main ideas as prescribed in the task or chosen by the candidate. Terminology is pertinent, is used correctly, and effectively conveys the intended meaning. Mechanics, usage, and grammar promote accurate interpretation and understanding.
could you do student.cpp
write roster.h

Certainly! Here is an example of how you might write the `roster.h` header file, which defines a `Roster` class to manage a collection of `Student` objects:

```cpp
#ifndef ROSTER_H
#define ROSTER_H

#include "student.h"
#include <vector>
#include <string>

class Roster {
public:
    Roster();                                    // Constructor.
    ~Roster();                                   // Destructor to properly handle memory release.
    void add(std::string studentID,
             std::string firstName,
             std::string lastName,
             std::string emailAddress,
             int age,
             int daysInCourse1,
             int daysInCourse2,
             int daysInCourse3,
             DegreeProgram degreeProgram);       // Adds a new student to the roster.
    void remove(std::string studentID);          // Removes a student from the roster by student ID.
    void printAll() const;                       // Prints all students in the roster.
    void printAverageDaysInCourse(std::string studentID) const;  // Prints average days in course.
    void printInvalidEmails() const;             // Prints all invalid email addresses.
    void printByDegreeProgram(DegreeProgram degreeProgram) const; // Print students by degree program.

private:
    std::vector<Student*> classRosterArray;      // Dynamic array of pointers to hold Student data.
};

#endif // ROSTER_H
```

A few notes on the Roster class:

- We are using a `std::vector` here to manage a dynamic list of students instead of a raw array of pointers. The `std::vector` has the advantage of managing the dynamic memory for you and adjusting its size automatically.
- The constructor will initialize the `std::vector`, and the destructor is responsible for cleaning up the dynamically allocated `Student` objects.
- The `add` function will create a new `Student` object with the provided parameters and add it to the `classRosterArray`.
- The `remove` function will remove the `Student` with the specified ID from the `classRosterArray`.
- The `printAll` method will loop through the `classRosterArray` and call the print method on each `Student`.
- The `printAverageDaysInCourse` method will calculate and print the average number of days across the three courses for a single `Student` identified by their ID.
- The `printInvalidEmails` method will go through all Student objects, check their emails, and print out the invalid ones.
- The `printByDegreeProgram` method will print all students enrolled in a specified degree program.

With this header file, you're setting up your `Roster` class interface. Remember to implement the member functions in the corresponding `roster.cpp` source file.
give me roster.cpp
can you do roster.h w/o vectors

Certainly! If you prefer not to use the `std::vector` from the C++ Standard Library and instead manage a fixed-size array manually, you can do it like this:

```cpp
#ifndef ROSTER_H
#define ROSTER_H

#include "student.h"
#include "degree.h"
#include <string>

// Assuming the maximum size of the roster is known in advance
const static int MAX_STUDENTS = 10;

class Roster {
public:
    Roster();    // Constructor
    ~Roster();   // Destructor to properly handle memory release

    void add(std::string studentID,
             std::string firstName,
             std::string lastName,
             std::string emailAddress,
             int age,
             int daysInCourse1,
             int daysInCourse2,
             int daysInCourse3,
             DegreeProgram degreeProgram);        // Adds a new student to the roster

    void remove(std::string studentID);           // Removes a student from the roster by student ID
    void printAll() const;                        // Prints all students in the roster
    void printAverageDaysInCourse(std::string studentID) const;  // Prints average days in course
    void printInvalidEmails() const;              // Prints all invalid email addresses
    void printByDegreeProgram(DegreeProgram degreeProgram) const; // Prints students by degree program

private:
    int lastIndex;  // Tracks the last index in the array that has been used
    Student* classRosterArray[MAX_STUDENTS]; // Fixed-size array of pointers to Student objects
};

#endif // ROSTER_H
```

Important points to consider:

- `MAX_STUDENTS` is used to define the maximum number of students that can be stored in the array.
- `lastIndex` is used to keep track of the last used index in the array, which will help when adding or searching for students.
- The `classRosterArray` is now a fixed-size array of pointers, which can hold up to `MAX_STUDENTS` number of `Student*` pointers.

The remaining implementation logic would be similar to what was previously discussed but would need accounting for the array's fixed size, such as checking for array bounds during add operations and appropriately managing the index counter.
do roster.cpp w/o vectors and if possible w/o sstream
do main.cpp

The errors listed seem to fall into a few categories:

1. **Syntax errors and missing semicolons.**
2. **Issues with accessing non-static members.**
3. **Function definition/declaration mismatches.**
4. **Uninitialized references.**
5. **Overloaded function resolution issues.**
6. **Mismatched function calls (functions not existing in given classes).**
7. **Undeclared identifiers.**

Without seeing the actual code, it's challenging to provide exact fixes, but I can give you some general advice on how to proceed:

1. **Syntax Errors**: Make sure all your statements are correctly ended with a semicolon and that all braces (`{}`) are correctly matched.

2. **Accessing Non-static Members**: Ensure that all non-static member variables and functions are accessed by an object of the class or via a pointer to an object of the class. For example, access using `myRosterInstance.lastIndex` or `this->lastIndex` inside member functions.

3. **Function Definition/Declaration Mismatches**: Confirm that the functions you're defining have declarations that exactly match in the corresponding header file (`Roster.h` and `Student.h`). All parameters, the function name, return type, and const-qualification must match exactly.

4. **Uninitialized References**: Ensure all variables that are references are initialized when declared.

5. **Overloaded Function Resolution Issues**: Make sure each function call provides correct types for parameters so the compiler knows which function to invoke.

6. **Missing Functions**: If the error says a function is not a member of a class (like `getLastIndex` or `getStudentAt`), you need to ensure the function is actually declared in that class's header file and defined in its source file.

7. **Undeclared Identifiers**: If something like `MAX_STUDENTS` is listed as undeclared, be sure it's properly declared in your header or source file where you intend to use it. If you've defined it in a header file, ensure that the header file is correctly included in the source files where you're using `MAX_STUDENTS`.

Let's cover an example for the `MAX_STUDENTS` and non-static members access. You need to ensure that `MAX_STUDENTS` is defined before you try to use it, typically in the `roster.h` file.
   
For the `lastIndex` and `classRosterArray` issues, you need to ensure any code that is trying to access them is doing so with an instance of the `Roster` class. Also, verify the correct syntax to access members of arrays, e.g., `classRosterArray[i]`. Remember to compile frequently as you resolve these errors to catch new issues that may arise after changes.

For function overloads, ensure that when you call a function, you provide the parameters that match one of the function's overloads exactly.

For missing or unreachable code (errors like C1075 and C2143), look for missing semicolons, braces, or other syntax issues.

Here's a simple example of what a correctly matched declaration and definition might look like:

```cpp
// In Student.h
class Student {
public:
    // ...
    void setStudentID(std::string id); // Declaration
    // ...
};

// In Student.cpp
#include "Student.h"

void Student::setStudentID(std::string id) { // Definition
    this->studentID = id; // Correct access of non-static member
}

// And in Student.h
const static int MAX_STUDENTS = 10; // Declaration of MAX_STUDENTS
```

Make sure you review your entire codebase carefully and match each definition with its declaration. Resolve one error at a time, recompile to ensure each fix works, and proceed systematically. This can often resolve multiple errors at once since one mistake in the code can lead to a cascading series of issues in the compiler output.
